coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of metal-free, yellow-emitting phosphor with organic single crystal as a matrix
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Shengju
Lu, Dandan
Sun, Xiaofeng
Yin, Keyang
Zhang, Geping
Chen, Mengjun
Li, Hongguang
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, there is a rapid growth on the research of metal-free phosphor. A typical example is blend of photoluminescent carbon nanomaterials with a matrix. Normally, the C dots were prepared first, which were then mixed with the matrix. A drawback of this strategy is that multiple steps have to be involved, which is time consuming. In this work, we show that by simply heating a readily-available small organic molecule (phthalic acid) in a high-boiling solvent with reactive group (formamide), ready-to-use and metal-free phosphor can be produced in high yields (over 70%). The phosphor contains single crystals of phthalic anhydride with photoluminescent species adsorbed on the surfaces, which shows yellow emission in solid state with a fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) of 28.53%. The phosphor can be directly used to prepare light-emitting diodes (LEDs), which results in a yellow LED on the UV chip and a white LED on the blue-emitting chip.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphor
Metal-free
Single crystal
Photoluminescent
Light-emitting diode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and composition of the phosphor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21875129 and No. 22002075 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Illustration of the preparation of the phosphor. b) A typical optical image of the phosphor. c,d) SEM images of the phosphor. e-g) Single crystal structure. The molecular packing as viewed parallel to the axis of the unit cell (e), hydrogen bonding interactions (f) and π-π interactions (g). The distances are in the unit of angstrom.

a) XPS spectrum of the phosphor with comparison to that of phthalic acid which acts as the starting material in current study. b,c) High-resolution C1s and N1s spectra. d) Optical properties of the phosphor in solid state. Inset is a photo of the phosphor under 365 nm UV irradiation. The dashed line is a guide for the eyes. e) Optical properties of the phosphor dissolved in DMF with a concentration of 3.0 mg mL−1. The dashed line is a guide for the eyes. f) Fluorescence decays of the phosphor at detected wavelengths of 511 nm with 400 nm excitation in solution, and at detected wavelengths of 568 nm with 470 nm excitation in solid state.

a) Photos of the phosphor obtained at different conditions. The numbering of the samples corresponds to that listed in Table 1 b) The phenomenon observed on the silica gel during the separation of the phosphor. The eluent is CH3OH/CHCl3 with a volume ratio of 1:9. c) Photos of the first two colored fragments dissolved in CD3OD under 365 nm UV irradiation. d-f) ESI-MS, 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra of the first two colored fragments, measured in CD3OD.

Illustration of the conversion pathways of PA and FA during pyrolysis. s: solid. l: liquid. I) Intermolecular condensation leads to the formation of PL species (C dots). II) Intramolecular condensation of PA leads to the formation of phthalic anhydride, which forms single crystals during cooling with C dots adsorbed on the surface.

a) Photos of the phosphor/PDMS hybrid film with a weight ratio of the phosphor to PDMS of 1:2.2. Taken under room light (left) and 365 nm UV irradiation (right). b) Photo of the LED lamp prepared with the phosphor/PDMS hybrid film and the commercially-available blue LED chip (λ ex = 460 nm). c) Variation of the CRI and luminescence efficiency of the LED lamp as a function of the applied current. d,e) The EL spectrum and CIE color coordinate of the LED operated at a current of 18 mA.
Influences of the concentration of PA (c PA), pyrolysis temperature (T) and time (t) on the yield and quantum yield (Φ) of the samples. The CIE coordinates of the samples are also included.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of metal-free, yellow-emitting phosphor with organic single crystal as a matrix

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Qilu Transportation, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Qilu Transportation, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, there is a rapid growth on the research of metal-free phosphor. A typical example is blend of photoluminescent carbon nanomaterials with a matrix. Normally, the C dots were prepared first, which were then mixed with the matrix. A drawback of this strategy is that multiple steps have to be involved, which is time consuming. In this work, we show that by simply heating a readily-available small organic molecule (phthalic acid) in a high-boiling solvent with reactive group (formamide), ready-to-use and metal-free phosphor can be produced in high yields (over 70%). The phosphor contains single crystals of phthalic anhydride with photoluminescent species adsorbed on the surfaces, which shows yellow emission in solid state with a fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) of 28.53%. The phosphor can be directly used to prepare light-emitting diodes (LEDs), which results in a yellow LED on the UV chip and a white LED on the blue-emitting chip.

Single crystals of small organic molecules with carbon dots adsorbed on the surface have been prepared. The hybrid material shows yellow emission, which can be used as metal-free phosphor for white-light-emitting diodes.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Lighting accounts for ~20% of global electricity consumption [1]. Light-emitting diodes (LEDs) have become an important class of energy-saving devices for the sustainable development of our society because of their small volume, long lifetime, and low energy consumption [2–4]. Rare-earth-based phosphors have been well developed and have been widely used in commercially available LEDs. However, they suffer from relatively high fabrication cost due to the usage of rare earth elements [5]. There are also some reports on LEDs based on semiconductor quantum dots (QDs). In this case, toxic substances such as Pd2+ or Cd2+ are unfortunately involved [6–8]. Development of environmental-benigh, metal-free phosphors is thus required which is beneficial for the sustainable development of the society.

In recent years, there is increasing interest in the research of photoluminescent (PL) carbon nanomaterials, among which carbon dots (C dots) represent a typical example [9–14]. C dots can be prepared by two strategies. The first one is called top-down method where carbon-rich bulk materials are cut into small pieces through acid reflux [15–27], laser ablation [28–30], electrochemical method [31–33] and sonication [34]. The second one, which is now more popular, is named bottom-up method where small organic molecules, synthetic polymers or biomacromolecules are carbonized through pyrolysis [35]. In both methods, a highly heterogeneous mixture will be obtained which contains C dots as well as various intermediates with a rather broad distribution of sizes and molecular weights. In most cases, the debris is also luminescent, sometimes even stronger than the C dots [36,37]. For water soluble C dots, they are normally purified by dialysis where the debris will be discarded. For this reason, the yield of the C dots is usually low, especially for the ones obtained by the bottom-up method. In rare cases C dots were also prepared in polar organic solvents and they were purified by precipation [38] or silica gel chromatography [39,40]. In these cases, the polarity instead of the size is the main criteria to sort the product.

Unlike semiconductor QDs, C dots contain only light elements such as carbon, oxygen, hydrogen and sometimes nitrogen and/or sulphur. They have low toxicity, tunable fluorescence emission and high thermal stability, and could be an alternative materials in new generations of LEDs. In addition, compared with the narrow bandwidth emissive commercial rare-earth phosphors and semiconductor QDs, the characteristic broad emission properties of C dots make it widely studied in the field of white LEDs [41]. However, C dots always undergo self-quenching in the solid state, which limits the wide use in LEDs [42]. Previously, phosphors were prepared by blending the purified C dots and solid matrices such as salt [43], starch [44], PVP [45], and silicone [46]. This two-step fabrication strategy will obviously increase the complexity and cost of the phosphor. Moreover, the incorporation amount of C dots is not easy to control, because too much or too little incorporation will lead to the decrease of fluorescence [47]. Herein, we provide a new methodology where a ready-to use phosphor was produced in just one-step. Using a standard method to produce water-insoluble C dots, PL species were produced by pyrolyzing phthalic acid (PA) in formamide (FA). The PL species mainly contain condensed small organic molecules with relatively low molecular weights. The content of C dots is negligible. Concurrently, a large amount of nonluminous o-phthalic anhydride was obtained from intramolecular condensation of PA. Interestingly, during cooling o-phthalic anhydride self-organizes into single crystals which can adsorb the PL debris, forming a ready-to-use phosphor. Unlike the production of other C dots where the organic fragments formed during the pyrolysis were obtained as by-products and were discarded, in current system both the PL debris and the nonluminous small organic molecule are involved in the phosphor, giving a high yield over 70%. More importantly, the matrix of the phosphor is a single crystal, which has not been reported previously both for organic and inorganic materials. This new type of metal-free phosphor shows yellow emission in solid state. Its application in LEDs was tested using both UV and blue-emitting chips.

Phthalic acid (99.5%), isophthalic acid (99%), terephthalic acid (98%), o-phenylenediamine (99.5%) and pyrocatechol (99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. PDMS elastomer kits (Sylgard 184) were purchased from Dow Corning (Midland, MI, USA). Epoxy-silicone resin, 395 nm emissive InGaN chips (emission peak at 395 nm, operating under voltage of 3.0 V) and 460 nm emissive InGaN chips (emission peak at 460 nm, operating under voltage of 3.0 V) were purchased from Shenzhen Looking Long Technology Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used without further purification. Solvents used are of analytical grade and were purchased from Tianjin FuYu chemical co., LTD (China).

Optical microscopy (OM) observations were performed on an AXIOSKOP 40/40 FL (ZEISS, Germany) microscope. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded on a HRTEMJEOL 2100 system that operated at 200 kV. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) observations were carried out on a SU8010 instrument. Before the measurements, the samples were sputtered with Pt in a LEICA EM ACE 200 automatic sputter coater. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a PANalyticalX’Pert Powder diffractometer (PANalytical, Holland) that operated in the reflection mode with Cu Ka radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250) with a monochromatized Al Ka X-ray source (1486.71 eV). Elemental analysis was carried out on an organic elemental analyzer (VarioEI III). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a VERTEX-70/70v FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Optics, Germany). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on a DSC 822e instrument (Piscataway, NJ, USA) with a scan speed of 10 °C·min−1. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Avance 400 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) spectra were recorded on a Q-TOF6510 spectrograph (Agilent, USA). UV–vis measurements were performed on a HITACHIU-4100 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with a scan rate of 600 nm min−1. Fluorescence measurements were performed on a LS-55 spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA). Fluorescence lifetimes were measured on an Edinburgh Instruments FLS920 with the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) method. For spectroscopy measurements in the solid state, the phosphor were ground into fine powders before use.

The as-prepared phosphor were first ground into fine powders. In a typical experiment, 0.3 mg of the powder was added to 2 mL PDMS followed by homogenization by stirring. The mixed solution was then dropped onto LED chips (GaN) and cured at 80 °C for 2 h. The mixed solution has also been engineered into films by drying within a clean petri dish for taking photos.

The phosphor was obtained via a standard pyrolysis process (Fig. 1 a). In a typical experiment, 2 g PA was dissolved in 10 mL FA, and the mixture was refluxed for 15 min. After the pyrolysis was stopped, a clear and yellow solution was obtained. The flask was then removed from the heating source and the solution was subjected to natural cooling. Interestingly, when room temperature is reached, we did not obtain a homogenous solution or solidified sample as usually observed in the pyrolysis of small organic molecules. Instead, formation of needlelike phosphor was noticed. When crystalization was finished, the phosphor occupied the whole volume of the solution and the sample became opaque. The phosphor can be redissolved in many organic solvents including FA, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), dimethylformamide (DMF), methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), acetone and ethyl acetate (EtOAc). However, they have poor solubility in water. To fully remove residual solvent molecules and other polar fragments possibly produced during pyrolysis, the sample was diluted by 40 mL deionized water and the aqueous dispersion was subjected to dialysis against water using a tube with the cut-off molecular weight of 1000 Da. During dialysis, the phosphor deposited within the tube. Finally, the phosphor were gathered and dried by lyophilization, which gives 1.437 g product with a calculated yield of 71.85%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and composition of the phosphor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1b gives a typical optical image of the phosphor. Note that during post-treatment of the sample, some big phosphor could be crumbled. Even though, the morphology of the phosphor is clearly seen. It was found that the phosphor mainly contain rods with diameters ranging from 30 to 180 μm. SEM observations on individual rods revealed that they have clear arris (Fig. 1c) and are solid (Fig. 1d). To gain insights into the internal structures, the phosphor were further investigated by X-ray single crystal diffraction. As presented in Fig. 1e–g, the X-ray single crystal structure of the sample is monoclinic with space group P 21/n. The crystallographic information is summarized in Table S1. The phosphor only include a single component, i.e., o-phthalic anhydride (Fig. 1e). A detailed investigation indicates that two types of interactions are present. One is hydrogen bonding interaction between the oxygen atoms and the hydrogen atoms at a distance of 2.585–2.625 Å (Fig. 1f). The other is face-to-face π-π stacking between two adjacent molecules at a distance of 3.443 Å (Fig. 1g). The detailed bond lengths and angles are shown in Table S2. These intermolecular, noncovalent interactions force the molecules to assemble in a highly ordered and multi-dimensional supramolecular framework.

The phase purity of the phosphor is sustained by the powder X-ray diffraction on the whole sample, which is in good accordance with the simulated result (Fig. S1). From 13C NMR spectra of the dissolved crystal (in DMSO‑d 6), the peaks can be well assigned to the carbons from o-phthalic anhydride (Fig. S2). These results, together with single crystal analysis, clearly indicate that the main component of the material we have obtained is o-phthalic anhydride. On the other hand, we have noticed that the sample has a yellow color, which is against the appearance exhibited by o-phthalic anhydride. Besides, the presence of nitrogen in the sample has been unambiguously confirmed by a variety of techniques. From XPS spectrum, three peaks were detected at 285, 400, and 531 eV, respectively, which are assigned to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2 a). Their atomic percentages are 71.52%, 12.06% and 16.42%, respectively. The C1s can be deconvoluted into four peaks of C–H at 284.2 eV, C–C/CC at 284.6 eV, C–N/C–O at 285.4 eV and CO at 288.3 eV (Fig. 2b) [48]. The N1s spectrum displays two peaks at 399.5 and 400.2 eV (Fig. 2c), attributed to pyridinic C–N–C and pyrrolic C2–N–H, respectively [49]. The O1s band contains two peaks at 531.5 and 532.3 eV for CO and C–O, respectively (Fig. S3) [49]. In FT-IR spectra, the N–H stretching vibration of the NH group at 3202 cm−1 and the C–N stretching vibration at 1382 cm−1 have been clearly detected (Fig. S4) [38]. Other evidences of the presence of nitrogen include elemental analysis, the energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra and SEM mapping, as summarized in Fig. S5–S7. These results indicate that besides o-phthalic anhydride, N-containing species are also present in the sample as the minor component(s). From the magnified SEM image of the crystal, the surface is rough and consists of irregular structures (Fig. S8), which might be formed by the minor component(s). Remarkably, the presence of nitrogen in the sample suggests that the solvent (i.e., FA) also took part in the reaction during pyrolysis. The peak at 11.35 ppm in 1H NMR spectrum correspond to protons on aldehydes [50], which further proves that FA took part in the pyrolysis reaction (Fig. S9).

The presence of the nitrogen-containing species makes the phosphor photoluminescent (PL), a property also absent for o-phthalic anhydride. UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and emission spectra of the phosphor have been examined both in solid state and in solution, as summarized in Fig. 2d–f. In solid state, the phosphor display an extraordinarily broad absorption, which covers the entire UV–vis region and extends to the near-infrared region (Fig. 2d). The absorption curve contains two obvious bands centered at 320 nm and 400 nm, which are assigned to the n
→
π* and π
→
π* transitions, respectively [51,52]. Under 365 nm UV irradiation, the phosphor display strong yellow emission, as seen from the photo shown in the inset of Fig. 2d. The PLE spectrum shows that the luminescence can be excited in a wide range with a maximum wavelength of 470 nm. The emission spectrum starts from ~500 nm and extends over 800 nm, which contains a main peak at 568 nm and two shoulder peaks at 531 nm and 611 nm. A CIE coordinate of (0.47, 0.52) was recorded, which locates in the yellow region (Fig. S10). The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (Φ) was determined to be 16.01%. When the excitation wavelength (λ ex) was varied, the intensity of the emission changes but its position remains unchanged, indicating that the phosphor can retain the yellow emission within a wide range of λ ex. When dissolved by organic solvents such as DMF, the π
→
π* transition can be still observed (Fig. 2e). The PLE spectrum ties in well with the absorption curve with the maximum wavelength also being 400 nm, suggesting that the emission originates from the band-edge exciton-state decay [53]. The luminescence can be excited with a wide range of wavelengths, and the maximum emission locates at 511 nm with a Φ of 38.41%. Similar with the case in solid state, a change in λ ex varies the intensity of the emission but its position remains constant. Compared to the emission in the solid state, there is a hypochromatic shift of 58 nm, which pushes the emission towards the green region (Fig. S10). Examination of the emission in different solvents at a λ ex of 400 nm shows that the emission is basically determined by the polarity of the solvent where a decrease of the polarity induces a hypochromatic shift (Fig. S11). However, the variation is only ~13 nm and all the emissions belong to the green region. The PL properties of the phosphor were further probed by time-resolved fluorescence measurements. Although the fluorescence lifetimes are short both in solid and liquid states, there is still some difference. As shown in Fig. 2f, the PL decay reveals that in DMF the data can be fitted by a mono-exponential curve with a lifetime of 5.11 ns, indicating that the excitons are highly stable and do not find nonradiative emission channels [54]. In contrast, in solid state the PL decay follows a bi-exponential fitting curve with lifetimes of 2.12 ns (43.02%) and 5.17 ns (56.98%), which gives an average value of 3.86 ns. The bi-exponential fitting curve may be due to the fact that some PL species start to emit light in the solid state.

The PL properties of the phosphor are heavily dependent on the content of the PL species which can be tuned by varying the parameters adopted in the pyrolysis, including the concentration of PA (c PA), the temperature (T) and the time of pyrolysis (t). To demonstrate these effects, additional ten experiments were performed as listed in Table 1 . Photos of corresponding products (denoted as 1#-10#), which were taken both at room temperature and under 365 nm UV irradiation, are given in Fig. 3 a. At t = 15 min, increasing c PA between 150 and 350 mg mL−1 (entries 1–4) leads to color fading of the phosphor, which can be ascribed to the rapid increase of the amount of o-phthalic anhydride that “diluted” the PL species. With increasing c PA, Φ first increases reaches the maximum at c PA = 300 mg mL−1 (22.28%, entry 3), and then decreases. When c PA is fixed at 200 mg mL−1, it was found that the content of the PL species is heavily influenced by T. When T is below the boiling point (m p) of FA (210 °C, entries 5–7), a quick fading of the phosphor both under room light and UV irradiation was observed, indicating that the pyrolysis becomes insufficient at low T. The content of the PL species can be also influenced by t, as exhibited by entries 8–10. At c PA = 200 mg mL−1, it was found that phosphor with good yields and quality were already produced within 5 min (entry 8). When t is increased, however, a rapid decrease of Φ was noticed (entries 9 and 10), which is probably caused by the too high concentration of the PL species in the sample that causes a pronounced aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) effect.

Despite of the obvious variations in the appearance and PL properties, all the products including the one we have discussed in Fig. 1 (denoted as sample 0#), have quite similar compositions with a nearly constant C/N ratio of 6.70 ± 0.07 determined by elemental analysis. This conclusion is affirmed by further characterizations by FT-IR, XRD and XPS on selected samples (Fig. S12–S14). By comparing the XRD patterns of all the samples (Fig. S13), it can be found that for the phosphor, apart from the prominent peaks of phthalic anhydride, a weak broad peak centered at around 24° is observed, demonstrating the existence of C dots in the crystals [55]. Single crystal analysis was also conducted on sample 10# which has the highest content of the PL species proved by its heaviest color, and exactly the same result as that of sample 0# was obtained. Imaging studies on typical samples revealed that the morphologies of the samples are also similar (Fig. S15 and S16). These results strengthened our conclusion that the PL species produced during pyrolysis is the minor component in the material, and the change of its content can hardly influence the composition and morphology of the whole sample.

To get further information of the PL species, sample 10# was subjected to silica gel chromatography using CH3OH/CHCl3 (volume ratio of 1:9) as the eluent. We first obtained a nonfluorescent band with a high Rf value, which was later assigned to o-phthalic anhydride. After that, a highly fluorescent band with Rf ≈ 0.80 was observed (fragment 1), which was followed by another two fluorescent bands (Fig. 3b). It is clear that the PL properties of the phosphor are determined by an integrated effect of all of these PL species. Following the nomenclature widely adopted in the literature, these PL species are assigned to C dots. The separation yield of fragment 1 is calculated to be 1.63%, and that of fragment 2 is 0.55%. Fragment 3 was not separated due to its extremely low yield. FT-IR characteristics of fragment 1 and fragment 2 are similar, but different from that of phosphor. Compared to the phosphor, the FT-IR spectra of fragment 1 and fragment 2 showed asymmetric and symmetric methylene stretching bands at 2930 and 2854 cm−1, which also proves that FA took part in the pyrolysis reaction (Fig. S17). The symmetric and antisymmetric O–H stretching modes at 3440 cm−1 have been clearly detected. As shown in the absorption curves of fragment 1 and fragment 2, the peak at 220 nm can be ascribed to the π→π* transition of the graphite sp2 domain (Fig. S18) [12]. When dissolved in CD3OD, fragment 1 shows yellow-green fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation, which is similar with the appearance of the phosphor in solution. Thus this fragment, which has a higher content, is assigned to the main PL fragment dominating the optical properties of the phosphor. Fragment 2 gives a blue emission (Fig. 3c), which is similar with the material obtained by solely heating FA at 240 °C (Fig. S19). Thus it could be the product of self-condensation of FA. The PL emission spectra of fragment 1 and fragment 2 at different excitation wavelengths were further studied. It can be seen that the emission peak of fragment 1 and fragment 2 did not shift under short wavelength excitation, which might correspond to the small molecule luminescence. The PL emission spectra of fragment 1 and fragment 2 have multiple emission peaks in the blue region (Fig. S20 and S21). This can explain why the prepared phosphor has only one emission peak in the solution state (Fig. 2e), but three emission peaks in the solid state (Fig. 2d). The small organic molecules may do not emit light at low concentration, but will only emit light at high concentration or in a solid state. Under long wavelength excitation, it showed a wavelength-dependent excitation behavior (Fig. S20 and S21), which corresponded to the C dots [56]. ESI-MS measurements give a variety of peaks up to a m/z of 1100 for fragment 1 and 800 for fragment 2 (Fig. 3d). The higher m/z values of fragment 1 compared to fragment 2 indicates that the former has larger size and/or a lower surface charge, which is also consistent with its lower polarity. Both fragment 1 and 2 show a variety of peaks between 6 and 9 ppm in 1H NMR spectra (Fig. 3e), which are assigned to the hydrogen atoms attached to the sp2 carbons. Correspondingly in the 13C NMR spectra, signals from sp2 carbons have been clearly detected between 120 and 140 ppm (Fig. 3f) [40]. Compared to fragment 2, signals from the sp2 carbons for fragment 1 have much stronger intensity, indicating that fragment 1 undergoes a larger extent of condensation during pyrolysis. This conclusion is also consistent with the results from HR-TEM. Under HR-TEM, it can be clearly seen that the main component of fragment 1 was C dots (Fig. S22). However, there were only few C dots in fragment 2, and more disordered structures were formed by small molecules (Fig. S23) indicating that it contains mainly PL debris with small molecular weights.

The story happened during pyrolysis is now clear. As shown in Fig. 4 , upon heating intermolecular condensation occurs between PA and FA, and in a less extent, among FA, producing a variety of PL species (pathway I). At the same time, PA itself undergoes intramolecular dehydration and forms o-phthalic anhydride (pathway II). Pathway II is dominant which produces a large amount of o-phthalic anhydride. In comparison, the intermolecular condensation only leads to a much smaller amount of the PL species. During cooling, o-phthalic anhydride becomes supersaturated, leading to the formation of single phosphor with PL species adsorbed on the surfaces.

It should be pointed out that the successful production of the phosphor is exclusive for PA. In control experiments, luminescent phosphor could not be produced when PA was replaced by its isomerides (isophthalic acid and terephthalic acid), o-phenylenediamine or o-dihydroxybenzene (Fig. S24). The possible reason is that the PA is more easily dehydrated to form five-membered phthalic anhydride with crystal structure. In addition, the choice of the solvent is also crucial, and replacing FA by solvents with similar structures such as DMF and DMSO failed to obtain the PL phosphor. The FA has a higher boiling point, which can provide a higher reaction temperature. In addition, DMF and DMSO are inert solvents and generally do not participate in the reaction, while FA has higher reactivity, which has been proven to play an important role in the formation of C dots by the literature [38]. In this system, FA is also involved in the reaction during pyrolysis.

The phosphor with good photoluminescence demonstrated in current work provide a new type of metal-free phosphors for applications in LEDs. Compared to these examples, the phosphor reported in this work was obtained in situ. The simplified methodology thus can significantly lower the cost of the phosphor, which is an important concern in LEDs. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) shows that the phosphor have good thermal stability with a decomposition temperature of ~150 °C (Fig. S25), which fulfills the basic requirements of the practical applications of LEDs. Sample 0# was selected for the following tests due to its high purity of the yellow emission and the relatively high Φ. The phosphor were mixed with polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) at different weight ratios (ρ). The film prepared by solidifying the mixed solution in a petri dish shows strong yellow emission. Fig. 5 a gives photos of the film prepared at ρ = 1:2.2 both at room light and under 365 nm UV irradiation. After the mixed solution was cast onto a UV chip with an excitation wavelength of 395 nm, a yellow-emitting LED was produced (Fig. S26). When the UV chip was replaced by a 460 nm emissive blue chip, a WLED was successfully prepared, as shown in Fig. 5b. The performance of the LED was investigated in detail within a range of applied current (I) from 2 to 200 mA (Fig. 5c, Table S3). While the color rendering index (CRI) increases continuously with increasing I, a non-monotonic variation of luminescence efficiency (LE) was noticed. The white light is to combine the blue light emitted by the blue chip with yellow phosphor. Therefore, such devices suffer from the change in CRI and luminous efficiency upon varying the input power, which is caused by the input power affecting the emission of blue light [57]. The electroluminescent (EL) spectrum at I = 18 mA is given in Fig. 5d, which corresponds to a white light with a CIE coordinate of (0.36, 0.39), a CRI of 74.5, an LE of 15.21 lmW−1 and a color temperature (T c) of 4748 K (Fig. 5e). This performance is comparable to the reported devices engineered by using metal-free phosphors [58], which provides a new type of devices for in office and outdoor lighting.

We have also prepared LEDs by using hybrid films with different ρ, and the results are summarized in Tables S4 and S5 and Fig. S27. Compared to the LED prepared at ρ = 1:2.2, the device prepared at a lower ρ (1:1.25) shows a lower CRI when I is below 10 mA, after which a comparable value was obtained. However, this device suffers from a low LE within the whole tested I. For the device prepared at a higher ρ (1:3), a comparable LE was obtained, but the device suffers from a much lower CRI. From Table 1 and Fig. 3a, one can see that samples 3#-5# have slightly higher Φ compared to sample 0#. However, their emission shows some green components proved by both naked eyes and CIE coordinates (Fig. S28). In addition, these three samples suffer from low contents of the PL species proved by their weaker color. Sample 5# with the highest Φ was also engineered into LED at a ρ of 1:2.2, which gives a blue emission at I = 18 mA (Fig. S29) due to the too weak absorption of the film on the blue light from the chip. Thus, to obtain a LED with good quality, both a high Φ and suitable content of the PL species in the phosphor are needed. A comparison with the existing literatures is presented in Table S6. These results are surely solid proofs of the comparable luminescence properties of the phosphor in WLED devices. At present, one-pot synthesis for large-scale production of C dot-based phosphor with high yields of the long wavelength emission beyond the blue light region remain a challenge [12]. The metal-free phosphor can be synthesized in a simple and green way with a low cost, giving it great potential in the sustainable application of LED. Considering that further engineering of both the phosphor and the phosphor/PDMS hybrid films is still open, the performance of the device could be further improved. Efforts towards this direction is currently underway in our lab.

In summary, we have developed a strategy to prepare ready-to-use and metal-free phosphor by one-step pyrolysis of PA in FA. Both the starting material (i.e., PA) and the solvent (i.e., FA) are readily-available, and the phosphor can be produced in large quantity thanks to the high yield during pyrolysis and the simple procedures involved in post-treatments. The phosphor is thermally stable in solid state up to 150 °C and shows yellow or yellow-green emission with Φ up to 28.53%, which allows us to develop a new type of LEDs. A yellow-emitting LED and a WLED were realized by using the yellow-emitting phosphor on UV and blue LED chips, respectively. At an applied current of 18 mA, the WLED shows a CIE coordinate of (0.36, 0.39), a CRI of 74.5, an LE of 15.21 lmW−1 and a Tc of 4748 K, which provides new choices for solid-state lighting. In addition, the phosphor is also highly luminescent in solution with a green emission and improved Φ. Although not presented in current work, the good PL properties of the phosphor may also find applications in other fields such as PL inks and anti-counterfeiting. We anticipate that this work will expedite developing new phosphors with different colors by adding other precursors in the preparation process. The related work is underway in our laboratory.

Shengju Zhou: prepared the yellow-emitting crystals and performed most of the characterizations. Dandan Lu: prepared the yellow-emitting crystals and performed most of the characterizations. Xiaofeng Sun: designed the project, discussed the results and wrote the paper. Keyang Yin: prepared the yellow-emitting crystals and performed most of the characterizations. Geping Zhang: prepared the yellow-emitting crystals and performed most of the characterizations. Mengjun Chen: prepared the yellow-emitting crystals and performed most of the characterizations. Hongguang Li: designed the project, discussed the results and wrote the paper. All authors contributed to the general discussion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21875129 and No. 22002075).
